label: πίστις Χριστοῦ — Faithfulness of Christ or Faith in Christ?
construction: Genitive phrase: πίστις + genitive proper noun (Χριστοῦ, Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ)

position_a.label: Objective Genitive — our faith directed at Christ
position_a.rendering: faith in Christ Jesus
position_a.argument: Greek genitives with abstract nouns like πίστις most naturally express the object of that noun. 'Faith of Christ' = 'faith aimed at Christ,' as in 'love of God' = 'love for God.' This reading is dominant in Protestant soteriology: we are justified by the act of believing in Christ.

position_b.label: Subjective Genitive — Christ's own faithfulness
position_b.rendering: the faithfulness of Jesus Christ
position_b.argument: Genitives with animate nouns naturally read as subjective ('the love of God' = God's love). Paul's contrast is never 'faith vs. works' but 'Christ's faithfulness vs. human works.' The phrase describes Christ's covenant obedience — his faithfulness unto death — as the basis of justification, not our faith-act. Romans 3:22 would not redundantly add 'for all who believe' if πίστις Χριστοῦ already meant 'believing in Christ.'

why_it_matters: If subjective genitive: justification rests primarily on Christ's own covenant faithfulness (his life, death, resurrection as an act of obedience), with our faith as response — not the meritorious act. If objective genitive: our faith-act is the instrument Paul is highlighting. The difference shifts whether Pauline justification is primarily Christocentric (Christ's doing) or anthropocentric (our believing). Both readings are orthodox but have different pastoral and doctrinal implications.

label: μονογενής — Only-Begotten or Unique/One-of-a-Kind?
construction: Adjective applied to the Son of God

position_a.label: Only-Begotten — from μόνος + γεννάω (to beget)
position_a.rendering: only-begotten Son
position_a.argument: The traditional etymology: μονογενής = μόνος (only) + γεννάω (to beget/generate). Adopted by the Nicene Creed: 'begotten, not made.' Irenaeus, Tertullian, Origen, and the Creed all read this as referring to eternal generation from the Father. The Latin translation unigenitus (only-begotten) follows this reading.

position_b.label: Unique / One-of-a-Kind — from μόνος + γένος (kind/class)
position_b.rendering: one and only Son / unique Son
position_b.argument: Modern lexicography (BDAG, LSJ) derives μονογενής from γένος (kind/class), not γεννάω (to beget). It means 'one of a kind,' the only one in its class. The LXX uses it for Jephthah's 'only' daughter (Judg 11:34) with no begetting connotation. 'Unique Son' captures the exclusivity without importing the eternal-generation doctrine from etymology alone.

why_it_matters: The Arian controversy turned partly on this word: Arius claimed the Son was 'begotten' (= created), Nicaea countered with 'begotten not made.' The etymological debate doesn't resolve the Trinitarian question but affects how English readers understand John's Christology: is Jesus described primarily as the eternally-generated Son, or as the incomparably unique one?

label: θεὸς ἦν ὁ λόγος — 'The Word was God' or 'a god'?
construction: Anarthrous predicate nominative before the copula: θεὸς ἦν ὁ λόγος

position_a.label: Definite — the Word was fully God
position_a.rendering: the Word was God
position_a.argument: Colwell's Rule (1933): definite predicate nominatives preceding the verb tend to lack the article. θεός here is qualitative-definite — not 'a god' but sharing the full divine nature of ὁ θεός. The anarthrous form distinguishes the Word (ὁ λόγος, articular) from the Father (πρὸς τὸν θεόν) without subordinating him. The context makes 'divine nature' the point: the Word shares the being of God while being a distinct person.

position_b.label: Indefinite — 'a god' (Arian/Jehovah's Witness reading)
position_b.rendering: the Word was a god
position_b.argument: When Greek lacks the article, the noun is technically indefinite ('a god'). If the author meant 'God' in the full sense, he would have written ὁ θεός. This reading sees the Word as a lesser divine being — the first creation, the chief agent of God, but not coequal with the Father.

why_it_matters: This is the single most debated clause in Christian theology. The anarthrous construction is grammatically regular for predicate nominatives (Colwell's Rule), making the 'indefinite' reading a false inference from English grammar mapped onto Greek. Almost all Greek grammarians (including non-evangelical scholars) reject the NWT's 'a god.' The verse is a high-stakes test case for how Greek grammar interacts with theological presuppositions.

label: ἐφ᾽ ᾧ Rom 5:12 — 'In Whom' or 'Because'?
construction: ἐφ᾽ ᾧ — preposition ἐπί + relative pronoun ᾧ (or conjunction)

position_a.label: Relative pronoun — 'in whom' (inherited sin in Adam)
position_a.rendering: in whom all sinned
position_a.argument: ἐφ᾽ ᾧ = ἐν ᾧ — 'in whom all sinned,' referring back to Adam. This supports Augustinian original sin: all humanity sinned in Adam federally or seminally. The Vulgate translated this in quo (in whom), which became the basis of the Western doctrine of inherited guilt.

position_b.label: Causal conjunction — 'because' (personal sin as consequence)
position_b.rendering: because all sinned
position_b.argument: ἐφ᾽ ᾧ is a fixed Greek idiom meaning 'on the basis that / because' (Philippians 3:12, 4:10 use it the same way). All humans die because all humans personally sin — death spread to all because all sinned individually. This reading does not require inherited Adamic guilt but emphasizes universal actual sin.

why_it_matters: The Western (Catholic/Reformed) doctrine of original guilt — that Adam's sin is imputed to all humans at birth — rests partly on this verse. The Eastern church, reading 'because,' instead emphasizes that Adam introduced mortality, and all who live in that condition personally sin. The difference matters for infant salvation, baptismal theology, and total depravity.

label: ἱλαστήριον Rom 3:25 — Propitiation, Expiation, or Mercy Seat?
construction: Noun used as predicate (God set him forth as ἱλαστήριον)

position_a.label: Propitiation — turning aside divine wrath
position_a.rendering: a propitiation / a sacrifice of atonement (wrath-satisfying)
position_a.argument: In pagan Greek, ἱλάσκομαι means to appease the anger of a god. The cognate ἱλαστήριον carries this force: Jesus absorbs God's righteous wrath against sin. Justification is possible because God's anger was satisfied. This reading grounds atonement in God's own justice requiring satisfaction.

position_b.label: Expiation — removing sin/guilt
position_b.rendering: a means of expiation / a sin-offering
position_b.argument: In the LXX, ἱλάσκομαι refers to purging or covering sin — not appeasing God (who is the subject, not object). C. H. Dodd argued that Hebrew כִּפֶּר (kaphar) means 'to cover/wipe' not 'to placate.' God is never the object of ἱλάσκομαι in the LXX. RSV's 'expiation' captures that Jesus removes sin, not that he redirects divine anger.

position_c.label: Mercy Seat (kapporeth) — place of atonement
position_c.rendering: the mercy seat / place of atonement
position_c.argument: ἱλαστήριον translates כַּפֹּרֶת (the lid of the Ark of the Covenant) in the LXX (Exod 25:17–22). Paul is identifying Christ with the mercy seat itself — the place where God meets sinners through atoning blood on the Day of Atonement. This reading makes the verse a direct typological allusion to Yom Kippur.

why_it_matters: The atonement theory implications are significant. Propitiation → penal substitution (wrath satisfied). Expiation → moral influence or Christus Victor (sin removed, no wrath focus). Mercy seat → participatory/typological atonement (Christ fulfills the Yom Kippur ritual). The Greek word is authentically ambiguous, and the OT background is decisive for interpretation.

label: Granville Sharp Rule — Titus 2:13 / 2 Pet 1:1
construction: τοῦ μεγάλου θεοῦ καὶ σωτῆρος ἡμῶν Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ — article + noun + καί + noun (no second article)

position_a.label: One referent — 'our great God and Savior, Jesus Christ'
position_a.rendering: our great God and Savior, Jesus Christ
position_a.argument: Granville Sharp's Rule (1798): when two singular personal nouns of the same case are linked by καί and the article precedes only the first noun, they refer to the same person. By this rule, 'God and Savior' is one person — identified as Jesus Christ. This makes Titus 2:13 and 2 Pet 1:1 direct ascriptions of deity to Jesus.

position_b.label: Two referents — 'the great God, and our Savior Jesus Christ'
position_b.rendering: the great God, and our Savior Jesus Christ
position_b.argument: Granville Sharp's Rule has exceptions and is disputed when proper names are involved. Some argue 'God' (ὁ μέγας θεός) refers to the Father and 'Savior' to the Son — two persons united in one salvation. The KJV takes this reading. Additionally, some early church authors read the verse in a binitarian, not modalistic, way.

why_it_matters: If the Sharp Rule applies, Titus 2:13 and 2 Pet 1:1 are among the clearest NT ascriptions of the divine title θεός to Jesus. The majority of Greek grammarians accept the rule for this verse. This matters for Christology: Jesus is called θεός (God) not just κύριος (Lord) in these passages.

label: עַלְמָה Isa 7:14 — Virgin or Young Woman?
construction: Hebrew noun עַלְמָה used as subject

position_a.label: Virgin — the LXX reading adopted by Matthew
position_a.rendering: a virgin / the virgin
position_a.argument: The LXX (Greek OT) translates עַלְמָה as παρθένος (virgin), and Matthew 1:23 cites the LXX explicitly. Matthew sees a double fulfillment: historical (a young woman in Ahaz's day) and typological/messianic (the Virgin Mary). The LXX translators in the 3rd–2nd c. BC may have understood the word as implying virginity in this context.

position_b.label: Young Woman — the Hebrew lexical meaning
position_b.rendering: a young woman
position_b.argument: Hebrew עַלְמָה (almah) means 'young woman of marriageable age' — not necessarily a virgin (Hebrew בְּתוּלָה, betulah, is the technical term for virgin). Genesis 24:43, Exodus 2:8, and Proverbs 30:19 use עַלְמָה without any implication of virginity. The sign to Ahaz was imminent (Isa 7:15 — before the child knows good from evil), requiring a contemporary referent. Matthew uses it typologically, not as the intended original meaning.

why_it_matters: The virgin birth doctrine is not dependent on this verse alone (Matthew and Luke independently attest it), but the translation shapes how readers understand OT prophecy and NT fulfillment. This is the most publicly contested biblical translation issue among Jewish-Christian dialogue.

label: בְּרֵאשִׁית Gen 1:1 — 'In the beginning' or 'When God began'?
construction: Preposition בְּ + noun רֵאשִׁית (absolute or construct state?)

position_a.label: Absolute state — independent clause, creation ex nihilo
position_a.rendering: In the beginning, God created the heavens and the earth.
position_a.argument: בְּרֵאשִׁית is in the absolute state, making 'In the beginning' a complete, independent adverbial phrase. Genesis 1:1 is a summary statement: God created everything absolutely and initially. This supports creatio ex nihilo — creation from nothing. Traditional reading of the Masoretic Text, supported by all ancient versions (LXX, Vulgate, Targums).

position_b.label: Construct state — dependent clause, 'when God began'
position_b.rendering: When God began to create the heavens and the earth — the earth being formless and void…
position_b.argument: בְּרֵאשִׁית may be in construct state (like בְּרֵאשִׁית מַמְלֶכֶת in Jer 28:1), making v.1 a temporal clause: 'In the beginning when God created…' This would be similar to Enuma Elish's opening ('When above…'). Verse 2 then describes a pre-existing chaotic state — not created in v.1. This does not necessarily contradict ex nihilo but changes the verse's grammatical function.

why_it_matters: The construct-state reading potentially implies pre-existent matter (a formless chaos in v.2 before divine ordering), which would challenge creatio ex nihilo. Most OT scholars today lean toward the absolute reading but acknowledge the construct reading is grammatically defensible. The question has implications for theology of creation and the relationship to ANE cosmology.

label: רוּחַ אֱלֹהִים Gen 1:2 — Spirit of God or Mighty Wind?
construction: Nominal phrase: רוּחַ + genitive אֱלֹהִים; participle מְרַחֶפֶת (hovering/sweeping)

position_a.label: Spirit of God — the Holy Spirit active in creation
position_a.rendering: the Spirit of God was hovering over the face of the waters
position_a.argument: רוּחַ + אֱלֹהִים is consistently translated 'Spirit of God' in the OT (Gen 41:38, Exod 31:3, Num 24:2). The participle מְרַחֶפֶת (hovering) appears in Deuteronomy 32:11 for an eagle brooding over her young — a maternal, personal image unsuited to a wind. The NT confirms the Spirit's role in creation (cf. Job 33:4; Ps 104:30).

position_b.label: Mighty/Divine Wind — an ANE cosmological idiom
position_b.rendering: a mighty wind sweeping over the face of the waters
position_b.argument: אֱלֹהִים as a superlative ('divine' = 'mighty') appears elsewhere in Hebrew (e.g., 'city of God' = enormous city). Wind sweeping over primordial waters is a standard ANE creation-myth motif (Enuma Elish). The NRSV and some critical commentators prefer 'a wind from God' / 'a mighty wind' to avoid importing the Trinity anachronistically into a creation text.

why_it_matters: The reading affects Trinitarian theology of creation and whether the OT itself reflects a differentiation within God's being. Most OT scholars who accept the Spirit reading acknowledge the theology is nascent — the explicit Trinitarian doctrine develops through progressive revelation. The wind reading is a minority scholarly position that sees the Spirit reading as anachronistic.

label: πληρόω Matt 5:17 — Fulfill or Confirm?
construction: ἦλθον πληρῶσαι — aorist infinitive of πληρόω + accusative noun

position_a.label: Fulfill — complete what the Law pointed toward
position_a.rendering: I came to fulfill the Law
position_a.argument: πληρόω in Matthew always means 'to bring to completion/fullness' (Matt 1:22; 2:15; 2:17, etc.) — never 'to obey' or 'to confirm.' Jesus fulfills the Law by: (1) embodying its intended meaning, (2) accomplishing what it typologically foreshadowed, and (3) inaugurating its eschatological completion. The Law pointed to him; he is its telos.

position_b.label: Establish / Confirm — Jesus affirms the Law's ongoing validity
position_b.rendering: I came to establish the Law / confirm its full meaning
position_b.argument: In Jewish idiom, 'fulfilling' Torah meant teaching its correct interpretation (as opposed to 'abolishing' = teaching it incorrectly). Jesus 'fulfills' means he teaches the Torah truly, in contrast to Pharisaic distortions. The antitheses that follow ('You have heard it said… but I say') are deepenings of the Law, not replacements.

why_it_matters: The question of continuity between the Testaments, the role of the Mosaic Law for Christians, and Sabbatarianism all hinge partly on this text. If 'fulfill' = 'bring to completion and transcend,' then Jesus can intensify and even transform the Law's application (e.g., dietary laws, circumcision). If 'confirm,' the Law remains fully binding in its OT form.

label: κεφαλή 1 Cor 11:3 — Head as Authority or Source?
construction: Metaphorical use of κεφαλή (head) in relational statements

position_a.label: Authority — head as ruler/chief
position_a.rendering: the husband is the head (authority) over the wife
position_a.argument: In both Greek and Hebrew idiom, 'head' (ראשׁ, κεφαλή) regularly denotes leadership, pre-eminence, or rule — not anatomical source. Judges 11:11, Isa 7:8–9 use ראשׁ for ruler/chief. BDAG (standard Greek lexicon) defines κεφαλή in this context as 'authority over.' The authority reading supports complementarian ecclesiology and marriage theology.

position_b.label: Source — head as origin/provider
position_b.rendering: the husband is the head (source/origin) of the wife
position_b.argument: Greek κεφαλή very rarely means 'authority' in secular literature (Liddell-Scott finds almost no clear examples before the NT). In Paul's context (Col 2:19; Eph 4:15–16), 'head' describes the source from which the body is nourished and grows — an anatomical metaphor for origin, not command. Berkeley Mickelsen and Catherine Clark Kroeger documented that 'source' was κεφαλή's primary metaphorical meaning in Koine Greek.

why_it_matters: This is the central grammatical dispute in evangelical debates over women in ministry and complementarian vs. egalitarian marriage theology. Grudem and Payne have debated this publicly for decades. The resolution requires both Greek lexicography and examination of Paul's actual head-body metaphors in context.

label: αἰώνιος — Eternal Duration or Age-Belonging?
construction: Adjective modifying punishment, life, destruction, etc.

position_a.label: Eternal duration — everlasting, without end
position_a.rendering: eternal life / eternal punishment
position_a.argument: αἰώνιος in the NT is routinely used for God's own nature (Rom 16:26) and for the age to come — both of which have no end. The adjective paired with life and punishment in Matthew 25:46 forms a semantic pair: if 'eternal life' is everlasting, so is 'eternal punishment' by parallelism. The early church consistently understood αἰώνιος as meaning 'without end.'

position_b.label: Age-belonging / age-quality — pertaining to the coming age
position_b.rendering: the life of the age / age-abiding punishment
position_b.argument: αἰών means 'age' (a bounded period), not 'eternity.' αἰώνιος is its adjectival form: 'belonging to the age,' 'age-characteristic.' John 17:3 defines αἰώνιος ζωή as knowing the Father and Son — a qualitative description, not a duration statement. N. T. Wright and David Bentley Hart argue the word describes the quality of the age to come, not unending temporal extension. This reading opens space for conditionalism or universalism.

why_it_matters: This word is the primary text for eternal conscious torment as opposed to annihilationism or universal reconciliation. If αἰώνιος means 'unending,' Matt 25:46 settles the question against both. If it means 'age-pertaining,' the word alone doesn't determine duration, and the question turns on other texts. The debate is active among evangelical scholars.

label: πρωτότοκος πάσης κτίσεως Col 1:15 — Over or Of Creation?
construction: πρωτότοκος + genitive πάσης κτίσεως

position_a.label: Firstborn over creation — preeminent ruler, not a created being
position_a.rendering: the firstborn over all creation
position_a.argument: πρωτότοκος + genitive can express supremacy or rank, not just birth into a group. Psalm 89:27 (LXX) uses πρωτότοκος for David's kingship over the nations — he was not born of the nations. Col 1:16 immediately clarifies: 'for in him all things were created' — Christ is the Creator of all creation, not a member of it. 'Firstborn of creation' would make him the first created being (the Arian reading).

position_b.label: Firstborn of creation — the first created being
position_b.rendering: firstborn of all creation
position_b.argument: The most natural Greek reading of πρωτότοκος + partitive genitive is 'the first one born within the group.' In Jewish Wisdom literature (Prov 8:22, Sirach 24:9), personified Wisdom is the 'first' thing God made. Colossians may be using a Jewish Wisdom Christology in which Christ is the highest of created beings — the divine agent of further creation.
proponents: James D. G. Dunn (Jewish Wisdom background)

why_it_matters: Arius cited this verse at the Council of Nicaea to argue that the Son is the first created being. Athanasius countered with Ps 89:27 and Col 1:16. The verse is still used by Jehovah's Witnesses to deny Christ's deity. The grammar is genuinely ambiguous — the theological context of vv.16–17 ('all things were created in him') is decisive for the orthodox reading.

label: εἰς ἄφεσιν ἁμαρτιῶν Acts 2:38 — For or Because of Forgiveness?
construction: εἰς + accusative noun (purpose/result/cause? — the εἰς is decisive)

position_a.label: Causal (retrospective) — 'because of' forgiveness already received
position_a.rendering: be baptized because your sins are being forgiven
position_a.argument: εἰς can express cause in Greek (Matt 12:41, eis to kérugma Iōna — 'at the preaching of Jonah'). Under this reading, baptism follows and expresses forgiveness already granted by repentance — a sign, not the cause. This avoids baptismal regeneration while acknowledging baptism's importance as response.

position_b.label: Telic/purposive — 'for/unto' forgiveness (cause or instrument)
position_b.rendering: be baptized for the forgiveness of your sins
position_b.argument: εἰς is almost universally purposive/resultive in the NT ('into,' 'for the purpose of,' 'resulting in'). The 'causal εἰς' reading is extremely rare and grammatically contested. Acts 2:38 most naturally reads: repent and be baptized → receive forgiveness + the Holy Spirit. Matthew 26:28 uses the same construction: Christ's blood is poured out εἰς ἄφεσιν — for forgiveness.

why_it_matters: This verse is the primary battlefield between baptismal regeneration (baptism causes or seals forgiveness) and baptism as sign/symbol (baptism follows and expresses forgiveness). The Greek is decisive for grammar: the 'causal' reading is almost certainly a theological, not grammatical, conclusion. Most NT scholars agree εἰς here is purposive.

label: γυναικί + αὐθεντεῖν 1 Tim 2:12 — Teach or Dominate?
construction: οὐκ ἐπιτρέπω γυναικί… αὐθεντεῖν ἀνδρός

position_a.label: Timeless prohibition — women may not teach or exercise authority
position_a.rendering: I do not permit a woman to teach or to exercise authority over a man
position_a.argument: αὐθεντεῖν means 'to have/exercise authority' — not 'to dominate.' The prohibition grounds itself in creation order (v.13) and the fall (v.14), making it transcultural. The Pastoral Epistles apply Pauline church order universally, not just to Ephesus. Women may not hold the teaching/ruling elder office.

position_b.label: Situational/semantic — prohibiting abusive authority or false teaching
position_b.rendering: I am not permitting a woman to teach or to dominate a man
position_b.argument: αὐθεντεῖν is a rare word — it appears nowhere else in the NT and rarely in Greek literature. Pre-NT evidence shows it often has negative connotations: to usurp, to domineer, or to kill. The situation in Ephesus (1 Tim 1:3–7; 4:7; 5:13) involved false teachers. Paul may be prohibiting women from teaching the specific false doctrines spreading in Ephesus — not establishing a universal ban. Philip Payne's exhaustive study argues the construction 'teach + αὐθεντεῖν' forms a hendiadys: 'to teach in a domineering way.'

why_it_matters: This is the most contested verse in complementarian-egalitarian debates. The resolution depends on: (1) the meaning of αὐθεντεῖν (authority vs. domination), (2) whether the prohibition is universal or situational, (3) how vv.13-14 (creation/fall grounds) function rhetorically. Both sides have produced major monographs. The Greek lexicography of a single rare word drives the entire debate.

label: ἁρπαγμός Phil 2:6 — What Did Christ Not Hold Onto?
construction: οὐχ ἁρπαγμὸν ἡγήσατο τὸ εἶναι ἴσα θεῷ

position_a.label: Something to exploit — he did not treat equality with God as something to use for his own advantage
position_a.rendering: did not consider equality with God something to be used to his own advantage / something to be exploited
position_a.argument: ἁρπαγμός can mean 'something to exploit/hold onto for one's own benefit.' On this reading, Christ already possessed equality with God but chose not to leverage it for advantage. The kenosis (emptying) was voluntary: he laid aside the exercise of divine prerogatives, not divine nature itself. This preserves full pre-existence and full deity.

position_b.label: Something to grasp at — he did not try to seize equality with God
position_b.rendering: did not regard equality with God as something to be grasped
position_b.argument: ἁρπαγμός in the active sense = 'act of seizing/robbing.' Christ did not grasp after or cling to divine equality. This may imply he had divine status but voluntarily relinquished its exercise ('gave himself up'), or that he, being in the form of God, refused to assert co-equality by force. Some see this as Christ's humility vs. Adam's pride in trying to grasp divinity (Gen 3).

why_it_matters: This verse is at the heart of kenotic Christology. How one reads ἁρπαγμός affects whether the kenosis means: (a) Christ already had and set aside the use of divine attributes (most orthodox kenotic view), or (b) Christ refused to grasp for equality he did not yet possess (more problematic for pre-existence). Hoover's 1971 article is widely considered decisive for reading (a).

label: σάρξ in Paul — Sinful Nature or Physical Flesh?
construction: Pauline antithesis σάρξ vs. πνεῦμα

position_a.label: Sinful nature — an inner orientation toward sin
position_a.rendering: sinful nature / flesh (as moral category)
position_a.argument: Paul's σάρξ is not the physical body itself but a moral orientation — the tendency of fallen human nature to resist God. 'Works of the flesh' (Gal 5:19–21) include sins that are not bodily (idolatry, jealousy, envy), confirming σάρξ is an ethical category. The NIV translates σάρξ as 'sinful nature' to capture this anthropological sense.

position_b.label: Flesh — physical, earthly humanity under power of sin
position_b.rendering: flesh / according to the flesh
position_b.argument: σάρξ means 'flesh' — the whole of human existence as a natural, earthly, mortal, conditioned reality under sin's domination. It is not a 'part' of the person (a 'nature' opposed to another 'nature'). Rendering it 'sinful nature' implies a bipartite anthropology (sinful nature vs. spirit) that Paul does not teach. The ESV, NASB, and most modern translations have reverted to 'flesh.' Paul's point is that 'flesh-oriented' life and 'Spirit-oriented' life are two different trajectories of the whole person.

why_it_matters: The NIV 1984's 'sinful nature' created a generation of readers who thought Paul was describing a 'sin nature' as a thing inside them. Fee and others argue this misrepresents Paul's anthropology and ethics — the Spirit vs. flesh contrast is about life-orientation under two different lordships (Sin/Adam vs. Christ/Spirit), not two inner natures at war.

label: ἐκκλησία — Assembly or Church Institution?
construction: Noun in various contexts (local assembly, universal body)

position_a.label: Called assembly — gathered community of believers
position_a.rendering: assembly / gathering / congregation
position_a.argument: ἐκκλησία in secular Greek = a civic assembly of called-out citizens. In the LXX it translates קָהָל (the assembly of Israel). In the NT it means the gathered community of those called by God — never an institution, building, or hierarchy. The word is active: it means the people assembled, not an organization they belong to. Most Baptist and Reformation traditions stress this.

position_b.label: Church — institution with apostolic succession and sacraments
position_b.rendering: Church (as institution)
position_b.argument: By the time of Ignatius of Antioch (c. 110 AD), ἐκκλησία referred to the institutional, episcopal church with bishops, presbyters, and deacons. Matthew 16:18 ('upon this rock I will build my ἐκκλησία') implies a lasting institution with authority ('binding and loosing') that transcends any single gathering. The Catholic and Orthodox churches see this institutional sense as the NT's intended referent.

why_it_matters: The distinction between 'assembly' and 'institution' affects ecclesiology, church authority, the papacy, and whether 'the church' can err. The word itself clearly means assembled people, not an institution — but whether that assembly has institutional structures (bishops, councils) with binding authority is the deeper ecclesiological question.

label: ἀγαπάω vs. φιλέω John 21:15–17 — Different loves or stylistic variation?
construction: Jesus asks ἀγαπᾷς με; Peter responds φιλῶ σε; third exchange uses φιλεῖς με

position_a.label: Meaningful distinction — two different kinds of love
position_a.rendering: Do you truly love me (unconditionally)? / Lord, you know I love you (as a friend)
position_a.argument: The alternation between ἀγαπάω (higher, sacrificial, willed love) and φιλέω (affectionate, friendship love) is intentional and theologically significant. Jesus escalates the question from ἀγαπάω to φιλέω in the third exchange — coming down to Peter's level. Peter never uses ἀγαπάω, perhaps because he dare not claim that level of love after his denial. This is the reading of many preachers and devotional commentators.

position_b.label: Stylistic synonymy — John varies words for literary variety
position_b.rendering: Do you love me? (same meaning both times)
position_b.argument: The Gospel of John systematically varies synonyms for stylistic reasons — the same phenomenon occurs with 'sheep/lambs' (πρόβατα/ἀρνία), 'feed/tend' (βόσκε/ποίμαινε), and 'know' (οἶδα/γινώσκω) in the same passage. BDAG notes ἀγαπάω and φιλέω overlap substantially in the NT. Demanding a theological distinction from word-choice that John uses as literary variation is over-reading the text.

why_it_matters: This is a classic example of a devotionally popular interpretation (two kinds of love) that most Greek scholars consider lexically unjustified. The lesson: Greek word studies must account for synonymy and authorial style. R. C. Trench's 19th-century lexicological distinctions have been largely revised by BDAG and corpus-based lexicography.

label: נֶפֶשׁ — Immortal Soul or Living Person/Life-force?
construction: Noun נֶפֶשׁ used in theological/anthropological contexts

position_a.label: Soul — an immortal spiritual component separate from the body
position_a.rendering: soul / immortal soul
position_a.argument: Greek philosophical influence (Plato's dualism) shaped much of Christian anthropology: the person is body + soul, and the soul survives physical death. Psalm 16:10 ('you will not abandon my soul to Sheol') implies a soul that God can preserve. This reading supports immediate personal consciousness after death.

position_b.label: Living being / whole person / life-force
position_b.rendering: living being / life / person / self
position_b.argument: In Genesis 2:7 God breathes into dust and the man became a living נֶפֶשׁ — the נֶפֶשׁ is not something you have, it is something you are. The word refers to the whole living person or the animating life-force. Dead people are called נְפָשׁוֹת מֵת (dead persons, Lev 21:11). Hebrew anthropology is holistic, not dualistic — the person does not have a soul, the person is a nephesh.

why_it_matters: The distinction between Hebrew holism (person as embodied whole) and Greek dualism (person as soul in body) shapes: resurrection theology, conditional immortality/annihilationism, intermediate state, and how one reads body-soul language throughout Scripture. N. T. Wright's 'Surprised by Hope' argues that the resurrection — not disembodied soul-survival — is the core NT hope, resting on this anthropological foundation.

label: צֶלֶם אֱלֹהִים Gen 1:26–27 — What is the Image of God?
construction: בְּצַלְמֵנוּ כִּדְמוּתֵנוּ — 'in our image, after our likeness'

position_a.label: Structural/Substantive — rational soul, moral capacity, spiritual qualities
position_a.rendering: the image of God as capacities: reason, morality, relationality
position_a.argument: The traditional Western reading (Augustine, Aquinas, Reformed tradition): the imago Dei consists in rational and moral faculties — the soul's ability to know God, exercise will, and relate morally. These capacities distinguish humans from animals and make humans bearers of divine likeness.

position_b.label: Functional/Royal — humans as God's royal representatives on earth
position_b.rendering: the image of God as commission: to represent God's reign
position_b.argument: ANE kings were called 'images of the gods' — visible representatives of divine authority. In Exodus, Moses sets up an image (צֶלֶם) that marks royal ownership. Genesis 1 uses royal enthronement language throughout: humans as God's image means they are his vice-regents, given dominion to represent his rule over creation. This is not what you have but what you do.

position_c.label: Relational — the image as existing in relationship with God
position_c.rendering: the image of God as relationship: to stand before and commune with God
position_c.argument: Karl Barth argued the imago Dei is relational — the 'I-Thou' relationship between God and humans mirrors the intra-Trinitarian relationship. The image is not a property but a posture — facing God, addressed by God, responsible to God. This reading grounds human dignity in covenantal address, not rational capacity.

why_it_matters: The imago Dei is the foundation of human dignity, bioethics, racial equality (Gen 9:6), and the theology of redemption as restoring what was lost in the fall. Different readings of the image produce different understandings of what sin damaged and what redemption restores — rational capacity, royal commission, or relationship.

label: μετάνοια — Repentance as Mind-Change or Full Reorientation?
construction: Noun μετάνοια and cognate verb μετανοέω

position_a.label: Change of mind — intellectual shift in thinking
position_a.rendering: repentance / change of mind
position_a.argument: The literal Greek etymology: μετά (change) + νοῦς (mind) = 'change of mind.' Some Word-of-Faith and certain dispensational teachers stress this intellectual/volitional sense: repentance means changing one's thinking about sin and Christ, not emotional grief. The intellectual change leads to consequent behavioral change.
proponents: Some dispensationalists who separate 'repentance from belief'

position_b.label: Full reorientation — turning away from sin toward God
position_b.rendering: repentance / radical reorientation / turning
position_b.argument: The NT usage of μετάνοια is decisively informed by the Hebrew תְּשׁוּבָה (shuva — turn/return). Repentance in the biblical sense is a whole-person turning: cognitive (recognize sin), affective (grief, 2 Cor 7:10 distinguishes godly grief → repentance vs. worldly grief → death), and volitional (behavioral change, 'fruits worthy of repentance,' Matt 3:8). The intellectual-only reading is a Greek etymology fallacy applied against the actual Hebrew and NT usage.
proponents: ESV, NIV, NASB, all major translations

why_it_matters: The 'change of mind only' interpretation is used in some quarters to separate faith from repentance and argue that repentance (as turning from sin) is not required for salvation — only intellectual assent. The majority scholarly view is that biblical repentance involves the whole person and cannot be reduced to cognition.

label: παρουσία — Second Coming or Presence / Visit?
construction: Noun παρουσία used for the coming/presence of Christ

position_a.label: Future Second Coming — a single end-time event
position_a.rendering: coming / Second Coming
position_a.argument: The NT consistently uses παρουσία for the future return of Christ — a universal, visible, climactic event. The word is used in the plural by some to refer to multiple visitations but in eschatological contexts always describes the definitive arrival of Christ. Most premillennial, amillennial, and postmillennial interpreters agree on the future referent.

position_b.label: AD 70 fulfillment — the coming in judgment on Jerusalem
position_b.rendering: presence / coming (in judgment, 70 AD)
position_b.argument: Full Preterists argue that Matthew 24's παρουσία was fulfilled in AD 70 when Roman armies destroyed Jerusalem — 'this generation shall not pass away' (Matt 24:34). In secular Greek, παρουσία meant the ceremonial visit of a dignitary or ruler. Jesus' 'coming' in judgment through the Roman armies fulfilled the Olivet Discourse within the generation that heard it.
proponents: Full Preterism (R. C. Sproul questioned partial preterism)
N. T. Wright (partial — AD 70 is the referent of some texts, but not all)

why_it_matters: Preterism represents a minority but serious scholarly position. The debate turns on whether 'this generation' in Matt 24:34 refers to Jesus's contemporaries (requiring AD 70 fulfillment) or a future generation that sees these signs. The resolution affects the whole eschatological framework.

label: γλῶσσαι 1 Cor 12–14 — Human Languages or Ecstatic Speech?
construction: Noun γλῶσσα (tongue/language) in charismatic contexts

position_a.label: Human languages — the gift of speaking identifiable foreign languages
position_a.rendering: languages / tongues (human languages, known or foreign)
position_a.argument: Acts 2 is the explicit description of γλῶσσαι: each hearer heard their own language (διάλεκτος). The word γλῶσσα primarily means 'language' throughout Greek. What Paul describes in 1 Cor 12–14 is the same phenomenon, possibly including languages unknown to the speaker but known to someone present. Cessationists often take this view.

position_b.label: Ecstatic/heavenly speech — non-human spiritual language
position_b.rendering: tongues (ecstatic, heavenly speech; language of angels)
position_b.argument: Paul says the tongue-speaker speaks 'to God, not to men' (1 Cor 14:2), 'speaks mysteries in the Spirit,' and his own mind is unfruitful (v.14). This resembles Jewish mystical traditions of heavenly language. 'Tongues of angels' (1 Cor 13:1) is taken literally. Paul's private prayer in tongues (v.18) does not require a human-language audience. Modern Pentecostal and charismatic practice reflects this understanding.

why_it_matters: The cessationism-continuationism debate (are the charismata still given today?) turns partly on what tongues was in the NT. If it was human languages, modern tongues-speech would have to demonstrate that character to be validated. If ecstatic prayer language, it operates differently. The interpretation also affects how 1 Cor 14's instruction on intelligibility applies to contemporary practice.

label: νόμος Χριστοῦ Gal 6:2 — Law of/from Christ?
construction: Genitive phrase: νόμος + Χριστοῦ (subjective or content genitive?)

position_a.label: Mosaic Law as fulfilled in Christ — the Torah interpreted through Jesus
position_a.rendering: the law of Christ (= Torah as fulfilled/reinterpreted by Jesus)
position_a.argument: Paul uses νόμος throughout Galatians to mean the Mosaic Torah. 'The law of Christ' = the Torah as it has been fulfilled, summarized, and reapplied in and through Christ. The law of love (Lev 19:18) is the summary Christ himself gave (Gal 5:14). This reading preserves continuity between the Testaments.

position_b.label: The new teaching/ethic given by Christ — a distinct law of the new covenant
position_b.rendering: the law of Christ (= Christ's own command or example)
position_b.argument: In Galatians, Paul argues forcefully against returning to the Mosaic νόμος. Calling the new covenant principle 'the law of Christ' deliberately contrasts it with Moses's law. The 'law of Christ' is Christ's own example of self-giving (taking up burdens, v.2) and his command to love — not the Torah reapplied. This reflects a new-creation framework that supersedes Torah.

why_it_matters: The debate touches on the Christian's relationship to the Mosaic Law: Is the Old Testament law still binding in its moral core (theonomic/Reformed view) or has it been entirely superseded by Christ's new ethic (Lutheran/dispensational view)? The phrase 'law of Christ' is a compressed Pauline formula where the interpretation determines the hermeneutical method for the whole Old Testament.

label: ὁ Λόγος John 1:1 — Pre-existent Person or Personified Attribute?
construction: ὁ Λόγος as subject of theological statements in the prologue

position_a.label: Pre-existent divine Person — the eternal Son who became incarnate
position_a.rendering: the Word (= the pre-existent Son of God)
position_a.argument: John 1:1–3 attributes to the Logos divine being ('was God'), pre-existence ('in the beginning'), and creative agency ('all things were made through him'). John 1:14 identifies this Logos as becoming flesh — incarnation of a pre-existing personal being. The Logos is not an attribute that became a person but a divine Person who always was.

position_b.label: Personified divine Wisdom/Attribute — a literary figure that became Jesus
position_b.rendering: the Word (= God's creative wisdom personified, now embodied in Jesus)
position_b.argument: Jewish Wisdom literature personifies God's wisdom (Prov 8:22–31; Sirach 24; Wisdom of Solomon 7–9). John is drawing on this tradition: 'the Word' is God's own creative, self-expressive wisdom — personified and then enfleshed. This does not require a separately pre-existing person; it reflects Jewish rhetorical personification of a divine attribute.
proponents: James D. G. Dunn (Christology in the Making)

why_it_matters: If the Logos is a pre-existent divine Person, the incarnation is the eternal Son taking on flesh — the classic Nicene doctrine. If the Logos is personified divine attribute, Christology develops more gradually in the NT (Dunn's thesis) and the incarnation is God's wisdom becoming embodied in a human being. The Nicene reading is the dominant scholarly and ecclesial position, but Dunn's challenge prompted a generation of Christological scholarship.

label: יֹום Gen 1 — 24-Hour Day or Longer Period?
construction: Noun יֹום with ordinal number: יֹום אֶחָד, יֹום שֵׁנִי, etc.

position_a.label: 24-hour day — literal calendar days
position_a.rendering: day (24-hour period)
position_a.argument: Whenever יֹום is used with an ordinal number in Hebrew it always means a literal day. The seventh day (Sabbath) structure of Gen 1 is the basis for the Jewish week — if the creation days are long ages, the weekly Sabbath has no grounding. Exodus 20:11 explicitly grounds the Sabbath command in the literal creation week. The evening-and-morning formula also implies literal days.

position_b.label: Age / era — an indefinite period of divine activity
position_b.rendering: period / age / era of God's creative work
position_b.argument: יֹום has a broad semantic range: it means 'daylight hours' (Gen 1:5a), 'a calendar day' (Gen 1:5b), 'an era' ('the day of the LORD,' 'in the day that God made' Gen 2:4 = the whole creation period). The 'day-age' view sees the six days as six successive long epochs. Framework Hypothesis: Gen 1 is a literary/theological framework (two triads of days), not a chronological sequence. Day 7 has no evening-and-morning — suggesting it is ongoing.

why_it_matters: The debate touches on the relationship between Scripture and natural science. Both sides agree the text is authoritative; the dispute is about genre and hermeneutics. John Walton's 'Lost World of Genesis One' (functional/temple inauguration reading) has reframed the debate significantly: the 'days' are not about material origins but about God assigning functions to his cosmic temple.

label: שְׁאוֹל — Grave, Underworld, or Conscious Afterlife?
construction: Noun שְׁאוֹל in various poetic and narrative contexts

position_a.label: Grave / death — the physical state of being dead
position_a.rendering: the grave / death / the realm of the dead
position_a.argument: In most OT contexts שְׁאוֹל simply = death / the grave — the state of not living. There is no clear OT evidence of conscious existence in Sheol. The dead do not praise God (Ps 115:17). Job, Ecclesiastes, and Psalms describe Sheol as a place of silence and ignorance (Eccl 9:10). Conditional immortality (soul sleep until resurrection) is more consistent with the holistic Hebrew anthropology of נֶפֶשׁ.
proponents: Many OT scholars on the linguistic data

position_b.label: Underworld — a place of conscious (if diminished) existence
position_b.rendering: Sheol / the underworld / the realm of shades
position_b.argument: Isaiah 14:9–10 describes Sheol as populated by shades (רְפָאִים) who rouse themselves to greet the king of Babylon — implying conscious (if diminished) existence. Ezekiel 32:21 has the 'mighty chiefs' speaking from within Sheol. Samuel appears and speaks from Sheol (1 Sam 28). The LXX consistently translates שְׁאוֹל as ᾍδης (Hades), the Greek underworld with conscious inhabitants.

why_it_matters: Sheol's nature in the OT shapes the intermediate state: between death and resurrection, are believers unconscious (soul sleep) or consciously present with God? The NT adds clarity (Phil 1:23; 2 Cor 5:8; Luke 16:19–31), but the OT data is genuinely ambiguous and has driven centuries of debate.

label: μαλακοί / ἀρσενοκοῖται 1 Cor 6:9 — Meaning and Application
construction: Two nouns in a vice list; ἀρσενοκοῖται is a NT hapax legomenon

position_a.label: Passive and active partners in male homosexual acts
position_a.rendering: men who have sex with men / homosexual offenders
position_a.argument: ἀρσενοκοῖται (ἄρσην = male + κοίτη = bed/intercourse) is a compound Paul likely coined from the LXX of Lev 18:22 and 20:13 (where both words appear). It means 'men who bed males.' Together with μαλακοί (the passive/receptive partner), the pair describes the two roles in male same-sex intercourse. This word was not coined for pederasty specifically — it is a general term for male-male sexual acts.

position_b.label: Exploitative/commercial contexts only — does not address committed same-sex relationships
position_b.rendering: male prostitutes / pederasts / sexual abusers
position_b.argument: μαλακοί = soft/effeminate (possibly male prostitutes, not all passive partners). ἀρσενοκοῖται is a rare word used in contexts of exploitation, economic abuse, and pederasty in post-biblical Christian writers. John Boswell (1980) and others argue Paul was addressing exploitative practices — not loving, committed same-sex partnerships, which were largely invisible in the ancient world.

why_it_matters: This is the most contested linguistic debate in contemporary Christian ethics. The word ἀρσενοκοῖται is central to whether Paul's prohibition covers all male homosexual acts or only exploitative ones. Robert Gagnon's work and the majority of NT scholars maintain that the word is general and not limited to pederasty. The debate requires both Greek lexicography and attention to Paul's creation-order hermeneutic.

label: ἐκπορεύεται John 15:26 — Eternal Procession or Mission Procession?
construction: ὃ παρὰ τοῦ πατρὸς ἐκπορεύεται — relative clause describing the Spirit

position_a.label: Eternal ontological procession — the Spirit proceeds from the Father eternally
position_a.rendering: who proceeds from the Father (eternally)
position_a.argument: Eastern Orthodox theology (Photius, Palamas): ἐκπορεύεται describes the eternal ontological origin of the Holy Spirit within the Trinity — the Spirit proceeds from the Father alone. Adding 'and from the Son' (filioque) to the Nicene Creed was the theological cause of the East-West Schism (1054). John 15:26 is the primary prooftext: the Spirit's origin is the Father, not the Father-and-Son.

position_b.label: Temporal mission procession — describes the Spirit being sent into history
position_b.rendering: who proceeds from the Father (on his saving mission)
position_b.argument: Western theology (Augustine, Aquinas, Reformed): John 15:26 is not making an ontological claim about the Spirit's eternal origin but describing the Spirit's temporal mission — sent by the Father and the Son into the world. Augustine argued the Spirit proceeds eternally from both Father and Son (filioque) on the basis of the perichoretic unity of the Trinity. The verse does not settle the eternal relations question.

why_it_matters: The filioque controversy divided Eastern and Western Christianity in 1054 and has never been resolved. Whether John 15:26 refers to eternal procession or temporal mission is the hermeneutical crux. Eastern scholars argue Western theology inappropriately imports eternal Trinitarian relations into a salvation-historical statement.

label: ἐπιούσιος Matt 6:11 — Daily, Tomorrow's, or Supernatural Bread?
construction: ἄρτον ἡμῶν τὸν ἐπιούσιον — hapax legomenon modifying ἄρτος (bread)

position_a.label: Daily / sufficient — bread for the present day
position_a.rendering: give us this day our daily bread
position_a.argument: ἐπιούσιος may derive from ἐπί + οὐσία (= 'what is needed for existence' → 'needful bread') or from ἐπί + εἶναι (= 'bread for the coming day' → 'daily bread'). Origen noted the word existed nowhere in Greek literature before the Lord's Prayer, suggesting it was coined or extremely rare. 'Daily bread' has been the virtually universal translation since Jerome's Vulgate (panem quotidianum).
proponents: KJV, ESV, NIV, NASB, all major translations

position_b.label: Eschatological / supernatural — the bread of the coming age
position_b.rendering: the bread of tomorrow / the bread of the coming kingdom
position_b.argument: If ἐπιούσιος derives from ἡ ἐπιοῦσα (the coming [day]), it could mean 'tomorrow's bread' — with an eschatological resonance: 'Give us now the bread of the coming age.' Joachim Jeremias argued the Lord's Prayer is eschatological throughout, and 'tomorrow's bread' fits: pray now for the provision of God's final kingdom.

why_it_matters: The word is a genuine hapax — it appears almost nowhere outside of Matthew 6:11 / Luke 11:3. Its meaning cannot be determined from parallel usage. Jerome himself said he couldn't find it anywhere. The 'daily' reading is most traditional; the eschatological reading opens the Lord's Prayer as a petition for the Kingdom's arrival. The ambiguity is irreducible without new manuscript discoveries.
